title: TPU v1 · Coding One PE: Combinational Multiply (=), Sequential Accumulate (<=)
oneLine: Each PE in the systolic array pairs a combinational multiply with a sequential accumulation: always_comb with blocking = computes the product, while always_ff @(posedge clk) with nonblocking <= registers the partial sum each cycle. Distinguishing these two forms and their assignment semantics is fundamental RTL discipline.

misuse can create races and mismatches.</text></svg>

legend.name: Combinational logic (multiplier: always_comb / =)
legend.desc: An 8-bit activation multiplied by an 8-bit weight produces a 16-bit product. This is pure combinational logic: it has no clock or state, and its output follows its inputs. Blocking assignment (prod = a*w) describes the dataflow and synthesis builds a multiplier.

legend.name: Sequential logic (accumulator: always_ff / <=)
legend.desc: At each rising edge, the 32-bit accumulator captures old acc plus the new product. A nonblocking assignment models parallel register updates; synthesis creates clocked flops that preserve state across cycles.

legend.name: PE inputs (a / w)
legend.desc: Activation a moves across the array while weight w is held for a weight-stationary dataflow. Both are 8-bit inputs to the combinational multiplier. The logic being combinational or sequential—not the input itself—determines whether = or <= is appropriate.

legend.name: Clock (700 MHz)
legend.desc: Only sequential state advances on clk; the combinational multiplier has no clock input. The PE's register-to-register multiply/add path must meet the 1.43 ns period at 700 MHz or be split by additional pipeline registers.

steps.cap: ① Combinational stage: a and w enter always_comb; prod = a * w uses blocking =. There is no clock or stored state, so the multiplier output follows the inputs.
② Sequential stage: prod feeds always_ff @(posedge clk), where acc <= acc + prod uses nonblocking <=. The expression reads the old acc and captures the new value on the edge.
③ Clocked progression: each rising edge advances acc and preserves it for the next cycle. Combinational logic computes within a cycle; sequential logic carries state across cycles.
④ The registered partial sum advances to the adjacent PE. The register both stores the accumulated value and establishes a clean systolic pipeline boundary.
⑤ Apply the discipline consistently: using sequential and combinational assignment styles incorrectly can create races or simulation/implementation mismatches—among the most subtle RTL defects.

before: Classify each block as combinational or sequential: ask whether it must remember prior-cycle state. The PE multiply is combinational; accumulation preserves a partial sum and is sequential.

Adopt one assignment discipline: use nonblocking <= in clocked always_ff processes and blocking = in always_comb processes. This is not cosmetic style; it preserves clear parallel-register and combinational-dataflow semantics across the design.

Define reset and widths: decide how acc is initialized and whether reset is synchronous or asynchronous. An 8-bit × 8-bit product needs 16 bits, while repeated accumulation uses 32 bits in TPU v1 to avoid overflow.
Use only synthesizable constructs: use always_comb/always_ff with complete combinational assignments. Keep delays, initial stimulus, and other testbench-only behavior out of design RTL.
during: Code the multiplier as combinational logic: always_comb prod = a * w; (illustrative) uses blocking = to express same-cycle dataflow. A combinational process must not depend on stored prior-cycle state, and every output must be assigned on every path.

Code the accumulator as a register: always_ff @(posedge clk) acc <= acc + prod; (illustrative) uses nonblocking <=. The right-hand side reads the pre-edge acc; all registers in the domain update together after the edge.

Do not mix the semantics casually: blocking assignments in clocked logic or nonblocking assignments in combinational logic can introduce event-order races, extra-cycle behavior, or mismatches between intent and simulation.

Make combinational sensitivity complete: always_comb automatically includes all referenced inputs, avoiding legacy sensitivity-list omissions such as always @(a) without w. Complete output assignment also prevents unintended latches.

Meet the register-to-register timing budget: the multiply/add logic must settle within a 1.43 ns period at 700 MHz. Systolic registers break a long computation into repeated short stages that can sustain frequency.

Keep the evidence boundary clear: prod=a*w and acc<=acc+prod are standard illustrative MAC RTL, not disclosed TPU v1 source. The published facts are the 8-bit multiply, 32-bit accumulation, and systolic organization.

result.label: Accumulator register (example HDL)
result.value: always_ff @(posedge clk) acc <= acc + prod;  // nonblocking <=, 32-bit

result.label: Core rule
result.value: Use <= for sequential state and = for combinational logic

result.label: Synthesized combinational stage
result.value: Multiplier/add logic; no clk and no stored state

result.label: Synthesized sequential stage
result.value: Clocked flops holding the partial sum

result.label: Single-PE timing target
result.value: Multiply/add path must settle within 1.43 ns at 700 MHz

after: Run lint for assignment-discipline violations: flag blocking assignments in sequential processes, nonblocking assignments in combinational processes, incomplete combinational assignment, unintended latches, and incomplete legacy sensitivity lists.

Simulate function and cycle boundaries: apply PE vectors and verify acc on every cycle. Toggle inputs around a cycle to confirm that combinational outputs respond within the cycle while registered state advances only on the edge.

Run trial synthesis and equivalence checks: confirm that the combinational stage maps to multiplier/add logic without latches, the accumulator maps to flops, and timing approaches 700 MHz. Compare RTL and synthesized behavior formally or with gate-level checks.

Check CDC at the array boundary: the PE itself is in one 700 MHz domain, but the Unified Buffer and host/PCIe interfaces may use other clocks. Verify the synchronizers and handshakes at those boundaries.
Hand off a trusted building block: provide the combinational/sequential partition, reset policy, widths, timing constraint, and testbench so verification can scale from one PE to the 256 × 256 array.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the combinational/sequential RTL examples follow standard practice and are not line-by-line TPU v1 source)
